CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well-organized (Intro → Method with clear subsections on Preprocessing, Attention, Comparison, Aggregation → Experiments → Conclusion) and states its main contributions early ("The contributions of this work are twofold..."). Notation is mostly consistent and definitions (e.g., Q ∈ R^{d×Q}, A, l, d) and the six comparison functions are presented clearly, and the equations in Sections 2.2–2.4 are informative. Clarity is reduced by numerous duplicated/garbled paragraphs and many referenced figures/tables (Figure 1, Table 1/2/3) are missing from the provided text, which together with a few minor notational/formatting ambiguities makes some parts harder to follow.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims (Sec.1) that a compare-aggregate architecture generalizes across four datasets and that element-wise comparison functions (SUB/MULT/SUBMULT+NN) perform best, and supports them with experiments on MovieQA, InsuranceQA, WikiQA and SNLI (Sec.3). The mathematical formulation and model equations (Eqs.1–9) are straightforward and appear correct. However, the experimental methodology has minor weaknesses: baselines are taken from reported results rather than re-implemented under identical settings (Sec.3.2), and no statistical significance testing is reported, which weakens some comparative claims. Overall these are implementation/analysis issues that do not invalidate the main technical conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper builds on an existing “compare-aggregate” paradigm (e.g. Parikh et al. 2016, match‑LSTM, He & Lin 2016) rather than proposing a fundamentally new framework. Its main novel contributions are empirical/systematic: evaluating six comparison functions across four diverse datasets and demonstrating that simple element-wise comparisons (SUB, MULT, SUBMULT+NN) work very well. Architectural choices (preprocessing gates, CNN aggregation, one‑way attention) are modest engineering variants rather than major methodological breakthroughs; some element-wise ideas were also used previously (Mou et al. 2016), so the contribution is best characterized as a useful, but incremental, empirical study.
